description: Fix 6 E2E test failures in CI (macOS + Linux). These are stress tests that found real platform bugs — port editor reconnect, reload notification pipeline, dispatch during registry rebuild, tab close detection, and tab state sync under rapid churn. For each failure, investigate the root cause: if it's a platform bug, fix the platform; if it's a test timing issue, fix the test with proper waits. The system must be robust under all conditions, not just local dev.

userStories.title: Fix side-panel-port-change stress tests — boundary values and same-port no-op
userStories.description: Two stress tests fail in CI: (1) 'boundary values are clamped by NumberStepper' times out at 49s, (2) 'entering same port does not cause unnecessary reconnect' times out at 33s. Investigate whether NumberStepper clamp-on-blur works correctly in headless Chromium, and whether entering the same port value triggers an unnecessary WebSocket reconnect.
acceptanceCriteria: side-panel-port-change.e2e.ts:94 stress test passes in CI (boundary values clamped correctly)
side-panel-port-change.e2e.ts:164 stress test passes in CI (same port = no reconnect)
If the platform has a bug (e.g., NumberStepper blur event not firing in headless, or port change handler not checking for same value), the platform code is fixed
If the test has a timing issue (e.g., needs explicit blur trigger or longer wait), the test is fixed with proper Playwright interactions
npx playwright test e2e/side-panel-port-change.e2e.ts passes with --retries=2
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/side-panel-port-change.e2e.ts (the stress tests)
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/Footer.tsx (NumberStepper component)
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/retro/NumberStepper.tsx (if separate)

Context:
These tests were added by a recent stress PRD. They pass locally on a Linux server but fail in GitHub Actions CI on macOS. The boundary test types '0' into the port input and expects it to clamp to >= 1 on blur. The same-port test enters the current port value and expects no WebSocket reconnect.

Investigation approach:
1. Read the NumberStepper component to understand the blur/commit behavior
2. Read the port-changed message handler in the background script
3. Check if the handler compares old vs new port before triggering reconnect
4. If the handler always reconnects (even for same port), add a same-value check — that's a real bug
5. For boundary clamping, verify the NumberStepper's onBlur handler calls Math.max(min, Math.min(max, value))
6. The test may need `await portInput.press('Tab')` instead of `await portInput.blur()` for headless compatibility

Constraints:
- Do NOT just skip or delete the tests — they test real user scenarios
- If the platform needs fixing, fix it
- If the test needs better Playwright interactions for headless, fix the test

userStories.title: Fix side-panel-reload stress test — rapid POST /reload spam
userStories.description: The 'rapid POST /reload spam settles to correct state without duplicate cards' test times out at 36s in CI. Investigate whether the side panel correctly processes multiple rapid plugins.changed notifications, or whether notifications get lost/coalesced incorrectly.
acceptanceCriteria: side-panel-reload.e2e.ts:190 stress test passes in CI
If the notification pipeline drops or coalesces plugins.changed incorrectly under rapid reloads, the platform is fixed
If the side panel's getFullState call races with plugins.changed, the race is resolved
If the test needs better waits (e.g., polling for plugin card visibility instead of fixed timeout), the test is fixed
npx playwright test e2e/side-panel-reload.e2e.ts passes with --retries=2
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/side-panel-reload.e2e.ts (the stress test)
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/App.tsx (plugins.changed handler, getFullState)
- platform/browser-extension/src/background/background-message-handlers.ts (bg:getFullState)
- platform/mcp-server/src/reload.ts (reload coalescing)

Context:
The test fires 3 POST /reload requests with 200ms between each, then checks that the side panel shows the plugin card. It times out at 36s waiting for the card to appear. The reload coalescing should produce exactly 1 discovery cycle, but the side panel may not receive or process the final plugins.changed notification.

Investigation approach:
1. Read the test to understand exactly what it asserts
2. Check if the side panel receives the plugins.changed notification after rapid reloads
3. The issue may be that the side panel calls getFullState() on plugins.changed, but the server hasn't finished updating its state yet
4. Or the WebSocket message is lost during rapid reload cycles
5. Add proper polling: instead of waiting for a fixed element, poll getFullState or the plugin card visibility

Constraints:
- The test must verify the side panel shows correct state after rapid reloads
- If the notification pipeline has a bug, fix the pipeline
- Do NOT just increase timeouts — find and fix the root cause

userStories.title: Fix hot-reload-dynamic manifest change during active dispatch
userStories.description: The 'in-flight slow call completes and new tool appears after manifest change' test fails consistently in CI (3 retries, all fail at ~19s). Investigate whether the dispatch pipeline correctly handles a registry rebuild while a tool is executing.
acceptanceCriteria: hot-reload-dynamic.e2e.ts:1474 stress test passes in CI
If the registry rebuild interrupts in-flight dispatch (kills the handler reference), the dispatch pipeline is fixed to either complete with the old handler or cleanly fail
If the test's timing assumptions are wrong for CI, the test is fixed with proper waits
The new tool MUST appear in tools/list after the manifest change
npx playwright test e2e/hot-reload-dynamic.e2e.ts --grep 'manifest change' passes with --retries=2
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/hot-reload-dynamic.e2e.ts (the stress test at line 1474)
- platform/mcp-server/src/plugin-registry.ts (replacePluginRegistry)
- platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-tool-dispatch.ts (dispatch pipeline)
- platform/mcp-server/src/file-watcher.ts (manifest change detection)

Context:
The test starts a slow_with_progress call (3s), then modifies the plugin manifest via writeAndWaitForWatcher to add a new tool. The in-flight call should complete, and the new tool should appear in tools/list. The test fails at ~19s, suggesting either:
1. The in-flight call hangs because the handler reference was invalidated by the registry rebuild
2. The new tool never appears because the file watcher didn't trigger a rediscovery
3. The writeAndWaitForWatcher helper's timing doesn't work in CI

Investigation approach:
1. Read the test and the writeAndWaitForWatcher helper
2. Check if replacePluginRegistry invalidates in-flight dispatch handler references
3. If the dispatch holds a closure over the old handler and the registry rebuild creates a new one, the old handler may become null/undefined
4. The fix may be to capture the handler reference at dispatch start (before the async operation) so the registry rebuild doesn't affect it

Constraints:
- The in-flight call MUST complete (success or identifiable error, not hang)
- The new tool MUST appear after the manifest change
- If this is a real dispatch pipeline bug, fix it — don't skip the test

userStories.title: Fix browser-tools execute_script on closed tab stress test
userStories.description: The 'browser_execute_script on tab closed mid-execution returns error within 5s' test fails in CI on macOS. Investigate whether the tab close detection is reliable across platforms.
acceptanceCriteria: browser-tools.e2e.ts:3310 stress test passes in CI on macOS
Tab close during execute_script returns error within 10s (relax from 5s if platform detection is slower on macOS)
Error message identifies the tab as closed/gone (not a generic timeout)
A fresh execute_script on a different tab works after the failure
npx playwright test e2e/browser-tools.e2e.ts --grep 'tab closed mid-execution' passes with --retries=2
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/browser-tools.e2e.ts (stress test at line 3310)
- platform/mcp-server/src/browser-tools/ (execute_script implementation)
- platform/browser-extension/src/background/ (tab removal detection)

Context:
The test calls execute_script with a 3s async operation, closes the tab after 500ms, and expects an error within 5s. On macOS CI, the timing may differ — the tab close event may arrive later, or the exec file cleanup may be slower.

Investigation approach:
1. Read the test to understand the exact timing
2. Check how execute_script detects tab closure — does it poll? Does it listen for chrome.tabs.onRemoved?
3. The 5s timeout may be too tight for CI — the exec file polling interval + tab removal event propagation may take longer
4. If the platform's tab close detection is slow, improve it (e.g., add a chrome.tabs.onRemoved listener that cancels pending exec results)
5. If it's just timing, relax to 10s — still proves it's not hanging for 30s

Constraints:
- The test MUST verify fast error return (not 30s timeout)
- Relaxing from 5s to 10s is acceptable if the detection path is genuinely slower on macOS
- Do NOT remove the test — it tests a real user scenario

userStories.title: Fix tab-state-sync 5-tab churn stress test
userStories.description: The 'opening 5 tabs and closing 3 immediately produces exactly 2 ready tabs' test fails in CI. Investigate whether the tab state machine handles rapid open/close correctly, or whether there's a race between onCreated and onRemoved events.
acceptanceCriteria: tab-state-sync.e2e.ts:441 stress test passes in CI
After opening 5 tabs and closing 3, exactly 2 tabs with ready=true are reported
If the tab state machine has a race condition (e.g., onRemoved processed before onCreated for the same tab), the platform is fixed
If the test needs to wait for intermediate state to settle between operations, proper waits are added
npx playwright test e2e/tab-state-sync.e2e.ts --grep '5-tab churn' passes with --retries=2
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/tab-state-sync.e2e.ts (stress test at line 441)
- platform/browser-extension/src/background/tab-state.ts (tab state machine, pluginLocks)
- platform/browser-extension/src/background/adapter-injection.ts (injection on tab update)

Context:
The test opens 5 tabs to a matching URL in a tight loop, then immediately closes tabs at index 0, 2, 4. It expects plugin_list_tabs to show exactly 2 tabs with ready=true. The failure in CI suggests the tab state machine doesn't handle the rapid open/close correctly.

Investigation approach:
1. Read the test to understand exact tab open/close sequence
2. Read tab-state.ts to understand the pluginLocks serialization and checkTabStateChanges debouncing
3. The issue may be: tabs are closed before their onUpdated/status=complete event fires, causing the extension to never register them as 'ready', so the 2 remaining tabs may also show as not-ready
4. Or: the close events interleave with open events, causing phantom tab entries
5. The fix may be to add a small delay (200ms) between opening all 5 tabs and closing 3, giving the extension time to process the open events
6. Or fix the platform to handle the race correctly

Constraints:
- The test MUST verify exact tab count (2) — not >= 2
- If adding a delay between open and close makes the test pass, that's acceptable — it still tests rapid close behavior
- If the platform has a genuine race, fix it

userStories.title: Fix browser-tools await fetch HTTP status test (pre-existing flake)
userStories.description: The 'await fetch returns HTTP status' test at browser-tools.e2e.ts:853 times out at 29.6s. This is a pre-existing flake, not a new stress test. Investigate and fix the root cause.
acceptanceCriteria: browser-tools.e2e.ts:853 test passes reliably in CI (not flaky)
If the test's fetch target URL is unreliable in CI, use a local test server URL instead
If the execute_script async fetch handling has a bug, fix it
npx playwright test e2e/browser-tools.e2e.ts --grep 'await fetch' passes with --retries=2
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/browser-tools.e2e.ts (test at line 853)

Context:
This test predates the stress testing effort. It calls browser_execute_script with code that does an async fetch and checks the HTTP status. The 29.6s timeout suggests the fetch hangs — possibly due to a DNS resolution timeout or the target URL being unreachable in CI.

Investigation approach:
1. Read the test to see what URL it fetches
2. If it fetches an external URL (e.g., httpbin.org, example.com), replace with a local test server endpoint
3. If it fetches a local URL, check why it hangs — maybe the test server isn't started
4. The exec file pipeline may also have a bug with async operations that involve network I/O

Constraints:
- Do NOT just increase the timeout — fix the root cause
- If the test fetches an external URL, make it local for CI reliability
- The test must still verify that async fetch works in execute_script
